{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Black Knights' Tango| |rd| |bd|qd|kd|bd| |rd|= |pd|pd|pd|pd|pd|pd|pd|pd|= | | |nd| | |nd| | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | |pl|pl| | | | |= | | | | | | | | |= |pl|pl| | |pl|pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= || }} =Black Knights' Tango= ===2...Nc6=== Black begins the game by developing both knights, keeping their central pawn options flexible. With the work of Georgi Orlov, this previously obscure opening has been given new life. White has a number of sensible replies, but the most common is 3. Nf3 to discourage the e5 break. Sometimes also called the Mexican Defense or Kevitz-Trajkovich Defense. ECO listing is A50. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. '''1. d4 Nf6 2. c4 Nc6'''
3 4 5 6
[[/3. Nf3|Nf3]]
e6		 a3
d6		 Nc3
g6		 e4
Bg7		 +/=
[[/3. Nc3|Nc3]]
e5		 d5
Ne7		 e4
Ng6		 Be3
Bb4		 ∞
[[/3. d5|d5]]
Ne5		 e4
e6		 Qd4
Bb4+		 Bd2
Bxd2+		 =
